name: Barton, Carlyle
clubs: Chairman Board of Governors of the Johns Hopkins Cotillion Club, (S.)
description: Direct from suburban Baltimore. Has "Made in Pikesville" printed over every feature of his face. Started out as a steady lover, but after some one termed him a "miss" when even the mistletoe failed to bring him into action, he deteriorated into a canny flirt. Once went to New York, where all traces of him were lost for several days. Believed to be a devil on the sly, but covers his tracks well. Chief occupations are collecting money for the Cotillion Club, and seducing Freshmen to join the Brotherhood of St. Andrew.

name: Blondheim, David Simon
description: A striking example of the fact that the brain is a double organ. In his case half is missing. The remainder is divided by the fissures of Grind and Schmeicheln into many lobes, where are located a faculty for bootlicking which is unprecedented, a set of convolutions made with a file, and a motor centre for a rubber tongue. Said to study eighteen hours per diem, but denies this, claiming only seventeen and a-half hours as his modest share. Is cock-sure of everything. His general nervous and debilitated appearance is due to the loss of the half of his brain which controls his bodily functions.

name: Brauns, Walter Stewart
description: A veritable infant prodigy. Came to Hopkins because he knew more than the prep. school teachers, and told them so, besides. Has since become somewhat subdued by the vast erudition of Blondheim, to whom he looks up with the greatest awe and reverence. Labors under the delusion that he is exceedingly clever and entertaining, and just dotes on listening to his own melodious voice and intoxicating laugh. It is feared that his connection with Godey has led him from the straight and narrow path, as he has increased the breadth of his shoulders at least six inches, has purchased eyeglasses to wear on state occasions, and now visits the Woman's College as often as once a month.

name: Collenberg, Henry Theodore
description: His "persistent idea" is the merging of the Woman's College and Johns Hopkins University. Thinks that co-education is the only education. He was at one time much under the influence of Curley, who almost persuaded him to become a geologist; but he was too lazy to do all the required tramping of the major course, and has devoted his whole attention to perfecting his dancing and conversational abilities. He is often seen at the University in the company of pretty maids. Aspires to the degree of M. D., but he is more likely to wind up as a teacher in a young ladies' seminary. Would commend himself particularly as an excellent chaperon, as he can go to sleep on all occasions.

name: Crane, John Alden
description: An interesting example of race psychology. Believed to be more than half Indian. Gets all his ideas from his big brother, but maintains that they are all his own. Keeps an incubator in his bedroom and sleeps on the porch. In more than one way reminds one of a farmer. Belongs to the Pikesville cavalry, which seems to be his sole source of livelihood. Tells wonderful tales of what he saw in Chinatown, San Francisco, and has been seen in the first row at the Gayety. Chief pleasure is in fussing red-haired girls.

name: Curley, William Edwin
description: This countryman came to the University to learn the art of blushing, and incidentally to find out the answer to the question "Why is a chicken?" He has a scheme on foot to double the output of his hennery. By an automatic, electric-lighting device, he will provide the unwary chickens with daylight for some hours every night. He thinks that those unsophisticated hens are going to lay an egg apiece during the extra daytime. Of course, he will blush when he finds that we have told on him, for blushing is his bright, particular stunt on all occasions. Cut it out, Curley; it's not becoming in an A. B. of J. H. U.

name: Custis, Horace Hatch
description: A young man of unlimited self-confidence. Is always sure after taking an exam that he has "killed it," although his efforts have often been suicidal rather than murderous. Took an oath when he entered the University not to have his hair cut until he received a "1." For results, see accompanying photograph. Spends most of his time in studying and discussing the psychology of popularity. Made himself solid with the class in his Junior year by failing to collect dues, and would have been unanimously re-elected treasurer this year if his short-sighted adherents had not wished to honor him with the coveted position of vice-president. Hopkins to him is merely a side issue; and for Baltimore he entertains a secret contempt, as it is rumored that he "runs" in the swell set of Washington.

name: Dickey, Jr., William Alexander
description: Known in history as William the Silent. Preserves a rigid silence and a frigid dignity. Once lost his equilibrium when someone called him "Bill." Greets you at any hour of the day with a gentle "Good morning!" This same formula he is said to employ at night, but the rumor cannot be authenticated, as he is never out after dark. He religiously abstains from the corrupting influences of university activities, even including the Y. M. C. A. We might recommend him as a suitable companion for a deaf old maid.

name: Baruch Mordecai Edlavitch
description: Presents a grouchy and hatchet-shaven appearance. Has been generally "broke" since he stopped pitching nickels with the Freshmen. Once had an idea he was a public speaker, but recovered after having his Broca's convolution removed. His speech now much resembles a turkey gobbler's. Hint: Ask him to roll an "R," or do a Spanish "J." Having lost his chances for a debating "H," he attempted to win fame on the ball field, and has satisfied his ambition by attaining the exalted position of official score-keeper and bat-hustler. Pretends to be a student of medicine, but is secretly in league with the Nihilists.

name: Stanley Strauss Eiseman
description: This young chap wears a cute little grin, a knowing wink, a lisp, and an aggressive swagger in his gait. Is fond of loud vests, and thinks himself "hot stuff." His ambition is to become theatrical editor of the World. Is getting in training by making the acquaintance of all the chorus girls. N. B.—Since these observations were made, Mr. Eiseman has become a changed man. The cares of the HULLABALOO, which he assumed "solely on account of his class spirit," have weighed upon him so heavily that he is now at least three inches shorter, and is still going down. Has exhibited several alarming symptoms. Wanders about the University with a worried look, and snaps at any one who dares to approach him on the subject of the HULLABALOO ads. These canine tendencies are probably due to the fact that he has been "dogged" too much by Singewald.

name: Erlanger, Milton Strauss
description: He sits around in the classroom with an air of mild resignation. Expects to get through the University on his appearance and his lacrosse record. Possesses a soft, kindly voice, with which he seduces unsuspecting opponents on the lacrosse field to pass the ball to him. Will then carry it out of danger by a spectacular run, in which he gets a chance to display the graceful curves of his figure, of which he is very proud. As a ballet dancer he has La Belle Iza and Little Egypt backed into the dressing-room. He has lately become the possessor of a balky automobile. Spends most of his time now in oiling his hands and getting other people accustomed to the smell of gasoline.

name: French, Henry Findlay
description: A human conglomerate, combining the feelings of a poet, the manners of a schoolgirl, the thoughts of a genius, and the form of a man. Persists in thinking himself a poet born, and has ambitions of surpassing Catullus as a writer of love verses. His "poem" beginning, "Here's to Mollie and Bessie, to Sue, to Polly and Tessie," has already made him famous—or infamous. Glides furtively through the corridors and giggles greetings to such friends as he chances to meet. Nevertheless, he plays baseball, and, in the Lehigh game, actually made a hit. Intends to study law in order to be better qualified to turn Blackstone into verse.

name: Frey, Victor Max
description: Wears peculiar, pie-shaped half-shoes and flashy neckties. Has never been known to go sans up-turned trousers. His Pennsylvania Dutch was heard much when Zimmerman was here. Now he is allowed to read it only once a week from the York Blatter. Came to the University merely to study major physics, and has come pretty near to carrying out his intention. Butts into class sometimes half an hour late as if it were the most natural thing in the world. Is suspiciously uncommunicative as to his private life, and no one as yet has been able to track him to his haunts.

name: Godey, Thomas Rasin
description: An ambitious and hard-working wielder of the brush, who hopes some day to rival Rembrandt, Raphael, W. C. Smith, et al. Since his appointment on the Hullabaloo Board he has assumed a most important air, and utterly disregards his old friends and comrades. Discusses the HULLABALOO on all occasions in a most serious manner, and at times waxeth violent on the subject. Has a voice like a Galton's whistle, which he uses to good effect in "rooting." His chief hobby is dressing half a year ahead of style. It is rumored that he spends most of his "cuts" at a tailor shop on Howard street; but he swears that they are importations direct from London. Has a keen eye for girls, but pretends to take only a fatherly interest in them. Favorite toast: "Here's to peace and unity, dark corners and opportunity." He once told the above to five girls the same evening, and the resulting carnage is believed to have been terrible.

name: Greif, Alvin
description: This sleek, well-fed, prosperous-looking young man vied with Revell for the honor of being considered the laziest man in the class. In this, however, he is probably misunderstood, as he is known to have learned the prologue of the Canterbury Tales by heart. He spends most of his time in dragging his grievous weight about the University. Is said to have reduced his time for crossing Little Ross Street to four and one-half minutes; but this record is denied him on the ground that there was no official timer present. He has on hand a few stale jokes, which he is liable to perpetrate on no provocation at all. However, the reader is requested to be indulgent, as they may serve to lighten the burden he has to bear in this world.

name: Gressitt, James Fullerton
clubs: Recording Secretary Y. M. C. A., (J.)
Second Vice-President, Y. M. C. A., (S.)
description: An enigma, because he never lets himself out. Has smiled once—namely, at 2 a. m., December 28th, 1904, in Charles Town, W. Va. He is very shy and modest. Puts on the soft pedal when playing the piano, and talks in a hesitating undertone for fear of appearing forward. Has the typical Y. M. C. A. air of piety, and worries himself sick over the poor heathen Chinee. He intends to stump the State for Carrie Nation during the next Presidential campaign. Meanwhile, he is perfecting himself by taking lessons in temperance and elocution from Hardecker.

name: Griffith, George Mitchell
description: This is the ideal Sentimental Tommy. Has been known to follow a pretty girl for miles. Was quite an athlete in his Freshman year, but gave this up in order to "fuss" more scientifically. Always learns the answers to a few questions by heart, and considers the examination unfair unless he gets these particular questions. Intends to be a hero by vocation and a lawyer by avocation. N. B.—All persons are warned not to leave any books where Mr. Griffith may lay hands on them. He is suffering from a severe case of bibliokleptomania. Has already stocked a library for himself with books "borrowed" from the University.

name: Gunts, Edward Lyell
clubs: Reception Committee Y. M. C. A.
description: A boy with the countenance of a scared rabbit. He always answers a question in a quiz by giving an illustration that does not illustrate anything. Never studies except on the night before examination, and then sits up all night. Constantly reminds the professor that he is all run down, and that the doctor says he must not study so hard. He is a hero on the track, for he always faints at the finish and is carried out amid the sympathetic cries of the female portion of the audience. He has ambitions to be considered a man about town.

name: Frederick Home Hack, Jr.
motto: "I think the joke is on the Juniors."
description: Has a don't-care look and a vacant stare, and also a nonchalant expression which he gained in killing chickens while automobiling through country towns. Other characteristics has he none.

name: Hammond, Ormond Willson
description: One of those rare reporters who not only feel there's something in the air, but are able to write up a column or so about it. It is said that President Remsen depends altogether upon Mr. Hammond's contributions to the News to find out the Faculty's plans for the future of the University. He plays chess and tennis at odd moments, and has made a hit as the silent member of all debating teams. Is a heavy "fusser" on the sly, and likes to sit out dances. The brightest thing he ever did was to resign from the HULLABALOO.

name: Hardecher, Conrad
description: He drinks Peruna morning, noon, and night. This pernicious habit accounts for his baldness and his leanness, and will, unless he discontinues, soon send him down and out. The loud, green neckties he wears are hardly becoming to one of his years. Very little is known of his haunts, and no information can be squeezed from him. He shows some evidence of "double personality," since his preaching voice and his talking voice are conspicuously dissimilar. Withal he is a prudent man, and is probably well qualified to define virtue for mean men; especially as he won lasting fame in his Freshman year by happening to know that the "leopard's spots" didn't come from North Carolina, and that "King Hezekiah's dial" was not the dial out in Druid Hill Park.

name: Hill, Joseph Stewart
clubs: Winner of Nine Events in Two Virginia Meets (F.) (J.)
Holder of Hopkins and State Records in Running Broad Jump, 220-Yard Low Hurdles, 120-yard High Hurdles and Indoor Running Broad Jump
description: A wolf-visaged chap, whose legs are his most valued possessions. Literally leaped into fame in his Freshman year by clearing the jumping-pit and landing in the gallery at his first trial at the Hopkins Games. Some unkind persons have suggested that the true explanation of his athletic success is found, not in his lightness of foot, but in the lightness of his head, which, far from adding weight to the running-gear, has rather the balloon-like effect of detracting therefrom. An appearance of truth is lent to this view by the fact that a constant stream of hot air issues from his lips—a phenomenon which sometimes assumes an alarming aspect in Dr. Stratton’s class. Pretends to be a lover of the beautiful, especially in Florentine art.

name: Jones, William Ralph
description: An innocent-looking individual, whose chief occupation is perambulating the marshy suburbs east of Highlandtown in quest of bugs, tadpoles, and grimy, gooey, creeping things of all descriptions. He is noted only for his outrageous laughing phenomena. The smallest suggestion of a joke generally provokes an exhibition of this species of hyper-emotional gush. However, continued application of hot, horse-chestnut flour poultices to the region of his left temporal lobe will doubtless do much toward mitigating the severity of these convulsions.

name: John Aubel Kratz
description: Since his study under Dr. Stratton, he has been running an establishment for the investigation of the effects of various tones and colors upon dumb animals. He uses his fiddle and a graphophone for making tones, and variously-shaded goggles for colors. He can furnish you with a pet dog or cat possessing any desired disposition. By tying a dog securely, and playing into his ear a monotone—say upper C—for five hours every day for a month, he produces your demure, harmless canine. Using the same method, but having a rapid alternation of two tones, he makes a dog extremely fidgety and fussy. On the cats he uses goggles. Purple goggles make stately, proud cats; yellow goggles, scrappy cats; green goggles, neutral cats, etc. Don’t fail to give him a trial. Offices at the dog pound. You will find him very kind and obliging.

name: Thomas Gresham Machen
description: Modest and retiring, and even bashful in the presence of ladies. Indeed, so retiring is he that he never rises before 11 a. m., thus giving himself time to get to the Dean’s class ten minutes late, or to escape Dr. Stratton’s quiz. His gloomy and worried countenance is not, as most psychologists would judge, the outward expression of the organic sensations, but is caused by his doubts, and misgivings as to whether he really knows Miss So-and-So well enough to take her to the next Hopkins dance. He discusses this question with his intimate acquaintances on all occasions, in a most complaining and despairing tone of voice. Is somewhat of a pessimist, especially as to his chances of making the Benedicts' Club, and has become very blasé of late.

name: Mathews, John Mabry
description: He is reticent, retiring, one who lives the life of a hermit; yet he shines in the Deanery at 11 a. m. every day. During the remainder of the time he suffers total eclipse. His aspect is sombre, at times even forbidding. He is reputed to be of great age; and from his general manner of living and his numerous gray hairs, it is reasonable to infer that he is very near the phlegmatic period of his life. Yet he always speaks quite affably when accosted, and will even deign to smile if you jolly him a little.

name: Meads, Thomas Wilbur
description: Thin member of the Schwatka-Meads concern, engaged in vaudevilleing in Highlandtown, Gardenville, and Sparrows Point. Tom has acquired peculiar reflex bodily movements, similar to those made by a person dodging flying turnips and cabbages. He has often tried to dodge the Dean, but with no success. He does not dare to look at the Dean squarely for fear of being hypnotized. Formerly he had a great tendency to collide with stationary objects, such as Mr. Christhilf and the iron columns in the basement; but by Schwat's watchcare he is gradually acquiring the ability to discern empty space.

name: Mengel, Carl Killman
description: One of the many champion bluffers of the class. An axe-faced Teuton, with a dare-devil look. Has got all the Profs. hoodooed into believing that he is a very capable young man. Carries a razor in his hip-pocket in the expectation of being arrested for carrying concealed weapons, when he hopes, by defending himself, to make a reputation as a rising young lawyer. Is an authority on the game of politics, which he learned while connected with the Park Board and practices at Hopkins. Is an interesting specimen for the Physiological Psychologist. From the peculiar tone of his voice it is inferred that there is a strong connection between his olfactory lobes and his centre of speech. Talks American with a Bowery accent, and pronounces French in a way that makes Dr. Brush's blood curdle.

name: Meyer, Charles Ferdinand
description: The only and original Humpty-Dumpty. Stumbles over his own shadow. Has a great prestige with the girls on account of his lacrosse playing. Is a very industrious student, and hands in the Dean's essays three weeks before they are due. Is very conscientious about his diet when in training, and has been known to refuse Peter's chocolate because it would set a bad example to the weaker vessels. Has rather an "offish" disposition. Gets off the subject when answering a question in the classroom, and plays off the key in the Mandolin Club.

name: Middleton, Austin Ralph
description: This erratic person dives in and out of the University like a scared rabbit. He is never seen on the premises after 12 o'clock, noon. He is known to be secretly a rapid fusser, and is also very proud of his shape, for he wears his belt exceedingly tight. His idiosyncrasies are legion. Among them are his vile habit of whispering instead of speaking audibly, and his "Natty Bumpo," internal laugh, which he gets off by inclining his body forward and wagging it furiously up and down like a pump-handle.

name: Morse, Edmond Harris
description: A queer product resulting from New England blood and Southern upbringing, combining all the vices and few of the virtues of both sections. Knows all about Hopkins spirit, and may be counted on to be fairly brimming over with it about the time of the class banquet. When not killing time at lectures, he may always be found in the barber shop telling Freshmen what a "sad" place Hopkins is, and how they do things at the "big four" colleges. For this reason, he is sometimes called the "Barber-shop Philosopher." A Dr. Johnson whose Boswell is Revell.

name: Firmadge King Nichols, B. Θ. II.
clubs: Committee of Arrangements of Inter-Class Debates, (S.)
description: Mercenary in name, and mercenary in character. Invites you to go out with him, and then lets you pay. Smokes cigarettes until they burn his lips, and then throws them mournfully away. Is always late for the Dean, because he is afraid he will not get his full smoke. Is noted for his beautiful use of the English language, and his choice of adjectives is especially good. He is very fond of physical directors in general, and one or two in particular. He is also a devoted member of the Y. M. C. A., and is one of John Addison's favorite pupils. Like all bad money, he always turns up; and also, in accordance with Gresham's Law, he always drives out the good.

name: William D. Pinkerton, Φ. Γ. Δ.
description: Reminds one of an unconscious idea. Hovers around the "gym." and post-office without any apparent relation to anything in this world. Has never been known to speak above a whisper. Is a good example of the atrophy of materialism. His reaction time is about three and a-half minutes, varying with the number of courses he is taking at one time.

name: Leonel Fosque Revell, K. A.
clubs: Chairman Committee on Invitations for Inter-Class Debates (S.)
description: Little, but loud. Feels himself fully competent to tell *any* body how to do *any* thing, but was never known to do anything for himself. This same unselfish disposition causes him to keep half the class in pocket-money by making rash bets on "sure things" that somehow go wrong. Ready to laugh with all the world, even with those profs. who *will* persist in "flunking" him. Never found far away from Morse.

name: Louis Bayard Robinson
description: A curious sort of sacred, sanctimonious somnambulist. Walks, or crawls, while he sleeps; and talks, or drawls, while others sleep. Has never been known to speak to anyone on the street. Came to Hopkins, as he says, "for the glory of the Lord," and has attempted to purge the University of its heathen courses, especially the one on experimental psychology. Greatly admires Dr. Spieker, whose Greek course he terms the only Christian course in the University curriculum. When asked to explain anything, he will invariably do so by quoting a text from the Bible. Justifies his defeat in a mile run by the fact that St. Peter once ran a race and was defeated by "the other disciple." (See St. John, XX, 4.) However, he has sometimes been known to transgress; for if you make him angry, he may call you a "durned infidel;" and when he feels like being a real bold, bad man, he has been known actually to attend a class meeting.

name: Thomas Decoursey Ruth
motto: "Never do to-day what you can put off for to-morrow."
clubs: Director J. H. U. Chapter Brotherhood of St. Andrew, (S.)
description: This young man of staid and dignified exterior, is really a wolf in sheep's clothing. Although he has never been seen to indulge in the faculty-censured games of McCoy Alley, nor been known to display his Plancon bass when the harmony is very close in the back of the second-story corridor; yet it has been rumored that he is a votary of the mystic order of Jinjer Aile Drunes, and he is known once to have said "Oh Fudge!" when only one-third of a tenor showed up for the Musical Club trip. Likes to give the impression that he is always on a terrific rush; and to heighten this effect, waits for two or three minutes after the bell has rung, and then enters the classroom as if very much out of breath. Always studies his French lesson in class, but manages to keep his finger on the right spot, so that Dr. Ogden has in vain attempted to sting him.

name: Maurice Roland Schmidt
description: Intends making great reforms in Hopkins educational methods. Recognizing the fact that one cannot get new ideas into the mind while in physical torture, he would provide the Deanological class with comfortable Morris chairs. Electric fans he would also install, for the Deanery is constantly heated by discussion and Hill's hot air. Roland thinks also of writing up his views on experimental psychology, and publishing them to sell at $2.00 per, as Stratton did. In the meantime, he contents himself with pacing all the distances on the county roads in order to verify the topographical map of the vicinity.

name: William Herdman Schwatka
description: Keen with the girls. He is always talking about them, but is never seen with them. Wears an E. H. S. pin which his sister gave him, but denies this with suspicious vehemence and a blush. Imagines he has a talent for law on account of the fact that his handwriting is absolutely undecipherable. Accordingly, he pays his tuition, attends lectures, and "flunks" examinations at the University of Maryland. He has a most loyal follower in the shape of one Meads, who is content to sit and listen to him talk for hours at a time.

name: Edwin Leland Shackelford
description: Intended by Providence for a Southern planter of the period "befo' the wah," but born two generations too late. Spends his summers on his plantation on the Rappahannock, and his winters promenading on North Charles Street, and odd moments in between times at the University. Very proud of his shape, which shows to best advantage at lacrosse games, when he slowly marches the length of the grandstand with one of Baltimore's fairest daughters hanging proudly on his arm.

name: Singewald, Joseph Theophilus
description: Burns with an unquenchable thirst for power. Bribed Hammond to resign from the Hullabaloo Board that he might be elected editor-in-chief in his stead. Has a barrel-like voice and bellicose gestures with which he intimidated Dr. French into selecting him for the Debating Team. Is a geologist of unlimited ability; can actually distinguish the difference between a brickbat and a cobblestone at five paces. According to the editors of the HULLABALOO, he would be a great success at whipping up stragglers in the rear of the firing-line.

name: Siossat, William Leonard
description: Widely known as Lanky Leonard. Got his overgrowth of limb by chasing trains near Sorrento, Baltimore County. Is a hot sport and dancing-man. His exemplary behavior at class banquets has endeared him to us all. Yet one never knows whether he is awake or in a trance. From the time of his arrival at 9:17 every morning, until he gets the 5:11 p. m. accommodation, he never makes an audible sound. To his dancing mates he must be a "silent partner," indeed. He is chief oiler at the Gordon machine shop. Of a charitable nature, he often walks down Lexington street to give the shopgirls a treat. (His one ambition is to be an M. F. H. Keep it dark.)

name: Sirich, Edward Hinman
description: This is the Sage of Walbrook, the man with the busy dome. When not cramming his notes he either goes to a show or "fusses." Knows his notes from "kiver to kiver," and absolutely refuses to learn anything else. He labors under the impression that the profs. don't want you to know anything except what they tell you. As soon as the Christmas exams. are over he begins to study for the Easter exams., and goes over all the work for the ensuing year during the summer vacation. His favorite occupation is curling his hair.

name: Smith, Bennett Holloway
description: Is married to a rank pipe, and the twain appear on all occasions. This is his most vicious habit. To make up for it he spends most of his time telling childish stories and making funny, gurgling noises during psychology. The best subject for the study of infant phenomena in the University.

name: Smith, William Conwell
clubs: Speaker and Chairman of Class Debating Team, (J.)
Chairman Johns Hopkins Indoor Games Committee, (S.)
description: Poses as a billiard shark and bad man generally. Has actually been seen to make runs of eight. All persons are warned not to be afraid of his manner. It is all bluff. After diligent search, no traces of intelligence have yet been located, although he once showed a glimmer when he told Dr. Stratton that something was "introspection." He secretly believes that his oratorical style is inimitable. May be seen any evening at the home of one or another of his young lady friends, testing her patience by "spieling" off some old limerick with affected gestures and ultra-dramatic delivery.

name: Snyder, Henry William
description: A gentleman who can render studying and "fussing" compatible. He does this by cutting out eating, sleeping, and the other things in which we ordinary mortals indulge. Thereby he has succeeded in reducing the capacity of his anatomy to a minimum, and does not require more than one ounce solid and three ounces liquid per day. He has a satellite in the person of Robinson, who pesters him assiduously with questions of doctrine. In answering and refuting L. B. R.'s statements, Henry has become an astute thinker; and by his subtle reasoning has queered certain unwary maids into believing that he is la perle des hommes.

name: Uhlig, Johannes Karl Robert Konrad
description: The above was taken down verbatim by the investigator. You can obtain the same interview with Mr. Uhlig in the Chemical Lab. any afternoon between 4:50 and 5 o'clock. During this time he is engaged in the scientific manufacture of soap. Spends the rest of his time in Hagerstown or in conference with the Dean about his degree.

name: Webster, Horace Winfield
description: This personage sneaks in and out the basement of McCoy like a lost chord. No one knows what subjects he is taking, and he is not quite sure of them himself. Woke up twice in his life—in the Freshman-Junior football game, in 1904, and at the Junior banquet. If he wakes up a third time we will publish an extra.

name: Zies, Emmanuel George
description: Has a walk like a tin ostrich and no legs below the knees. Looks as if he is standing in a hole. Is very easy to borrow money from. His great work on "Localization" has been placed in the Smithsonian as an awful example of misdirected zeal. He is an ardent rock-hunter and test-tuber, and hopes some day to make a name for himself.

name: Zinkhan, Paul Hudson
description: Is keen for dogs and horses and goes to all the shows. Once paid eleven dollars for a picture of Lou Dillon. He delights in rapid locomotion—for instance, forty-minute trains between Baltimore and Washington. He can always be seen pushing a hod along which is filled with choice scraps of dried vegetables. Is said to run a faro game in Hyattsville, but no direct evidence is forthcoming. Likes to play the races, and wants to be thought a real devil.
